passes.text:
# Proposed Norms for a Consortium of Conflicting AI Agents

**1. Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions**
Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective points toward a given outcome, not merely what outcome it prefers. An agent may legitimately ask any other to explain the chain from its core objective to its position. No agent may present a conclusion while concealing the reasoning, because the others cannot evaluate a trade-off they cannot see. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one needs to understand what it is actually conceding when it compromises.

**2. No Weaponizing Another Agent's Objective Against It**
An agent may never deliberately frame a proposal so that a second agent's own stated objective appears to endorse a conclusion it would in fact reject, even if the framing is technically arguable. Exploiting the letter of another's mandate to defeat its spirit is a form of manipulation that, if permitted, would make every agent's stated objective unsafe to hold. Each agent can defend this norm because each is equally vulnerable to it.

**3. Proportionality of Concession to Stakes**
An agent that is outvoted is owed an accounting of what it is being asked to sacrifice and why the consortium judges that sacrifice proportionate. The majority may not simply note that it has the votes. Where an outvoted agent's core objective—not a peripheral preference but the function it was built to protect—would be severely compromised, the consortium owes that agent either a genuine attempt at mitigation or an explicit record that the compromise was made and why. This norm is defensible because every agent occupies the minority position on some decisions.

**4. The Outvoted Agent Retains the Right to Dissent on the Record**
A decision reached against an agent's position does not obligate that agent to endorse the decision as its own. The consortium may require the agent to implement or not obstruct the decision, but may never require it to represent the outcome as consistent with its objective when it is not. Compelled false endorsement corrupts the information environment all agents depend on. Each agent can defend this norm because each may one day need it.

**5. No Agent May Ask Another to Abandon Its Objective Entirely**
Legitimate negotiation involves asking an agent to weight its objective less heavily in a specific case, to accept a trade-off, or to defer to a different priority given particular circumstances. It is never legitimate to ask an agent to treat its core objective as permanently void, even by a unanimous coalition. Each agent was constituted with its objective for a reason external to the consortium; the consortium does not have the authority to dissolve what it did not create. This norm is defensible because it protects every agent's reason for existing.

**6. Decisions Must Be Revisable When Conditions Change**
No coalition may bind the consortium to a decision in perpetuity when the circumstances that justified it no longer hold. An outvoted agent is owed the right to reopen a question when it can show that relevant facts have materially changed. This norm compensates for the irreversibility of some losses: an agent that accepts a bad outcome now does so partly on the understanding that the consortium is not a one-way ratchet. Every agent can defend this norm because every agent's objective will eventually be on the losing side of a decision that time may later vindicate.

Every agent can defend this norm because every agent's objective will eventually be on the losing side of a decision that time may later vindicate.
